items.q: What is the difference between a shift in demand and a movement along the demand curve?
items.correct: A movement along the curve is caused by a change in price; a shift of the whole curve is caused by changes in other factors (income, tastes, substitute prices).
items.misconception: A change in price shifts the demand curve.
items.random_wrong: Demand always falls when supply increases.
items.why: This is the single most common error in introductory economics. Price changes cause movements ALONG the curve; the curve itself shifts only when non-price factors change.

items.q: What does price elasticity of demand measure?
items.correct: The responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price — the percentage change in quantity divided by the percentage change in price.
items.misconception: PED measures how flexible or stretchy a product is.
items.random_wrong: PED measures whether a product is expensive or cheap.
items.why: Students take 'elasticity' literally. It is a measure of sensitivity — how much quantity responds when price changes. The product itself isn't 'elastic'; demand for it is.

items.correct: A sustained rise in the general price level over time — reducing the purchasing power of money. Measured by the percentage change in a price index.
items.misconception: Inflation is when prices are high.
items.random_wrong: Inflation is when the economy grows too fast.
items.why: Inflation is about RISING prices, not high prices. A country can have stable high prices (no inflation) or rising low prices (high inflation). Students confuse level with rate of change.

items.q: Which factor causes a SHIFT in the demand curve (not a movement along it)?
items.misconception: A rise in the price of the good itself
items.random_wrong: A fall in the good's own price
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A rise in consumer income. “A rise in the price of the good itself” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Quantity demanded equals quantity supplied. “Shortage of the good” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If demand is Qd = 100 - 2P and supply is Qs = 20 + 2P, what is the equilibrium price?
items.why: The real figure is 20. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
